content: Batch file rename collision pitfalls: the dangerous failure mode is silent collision — when a rename pattern maps two or more distinct source files to the same destination filename, most naive scripts just let the second write clobber the first with zero warning, permanently losing the earlier file's data. A documented real-world case: a bash regex capture-group bug silently produced empty capture groups for files with non-ASCII (CJK) names, causing ~38 files to collapse onto 2 surviving filenames with no error raised. Recommended safeguards: (1) always dry-run/preview the full old-name→new-name mapping before executing, (2) explicitly detect and hard-fail (never proceed) on any destination-name collision within a batch, (3) snapshot/backup the source directory before any destructive batch rename, (4) explicitly log and hard-fail (not skip-silently) on any filename/folder-name that doesn't match the expected pattern, rather than silently omitting it from the batch.
